entry.title: Group Reporting - Upload GL Accounts information via Flexible Upload
entry.content:
Dear Experts,

we load transactions (reporting) data into Group Reporting using flexible upload in Data Monitor. This works well so far. However, since we will only be release universal journal directly from ACDOCA starting next year, I would also like to load the GL account information for the current period using flexible upload. In other words, I want to load the FS item as well as the GL account information. Unfortunately, I get the following error: Field with GL Account value 56000 contains invalid value. FIN_CS_MESSAGE075

But in the Master Data for Consolidation Fields the GL Accounts 56000 is available. 

So I need your help how to load flexible data with GL Accounts field to have the information of GL Account in on my Documenttype 00 where we make some reclassifications with GL Accounts trigger in Data Monitor. We are using Group Reporting on public cloud.

Thanks and Regards

Daniel

entry.title: Re: Copying data from SAP to Excel
entry.content: After executing MB51, you can define a layout with only the columns you want, and you can use the Download excel option. If this is specific to some user, save it as a user‑specific layout. When you export or copy, only those columns are included, so excel won’t have gaps.

entry.title: DG Exceptions in ECC
entry.content:
Hi,

My question is almost two-fold. Firstly, we are using ECC currently and have a product that has DG exceptions under certain packaging. Currently our specification has all packaging types assigned under the Material Assignments, with one DG inheritance that applies to the specification as a Dangerous Goods across the board for the determined regulations. The packages with the exceptions have a material assignment on a separate DG_CL_SUB with the nuances. However, when customers are receiving SDSs via SDS Distribution, section 14 is not showing correctly. Is this because material assignments are assigned in multiple places? How can DG exceptions be managed?

 

Furthermore, how do we manage this moving forwards in S/4 HANA and also in the not-so-distance future of PC New?

I need to enable a check when filling out the profitability segment (FBS1):  I need to make the field mandatory only for some documents/G/L Account (Some fields are already mandatory for all documents).

To do this, I tried enabling validation (GGB0 with exit), but when I debug the exit code, I don't see any structure containing the fields I entered.

What can I do? Can you help me?

Thanks

Andrea

entry.title: How to use BPA API Trigger using Fiori
entry.content:
Introduction: How to make your Fiori app talk to SAP Build Process Automation (BPA)? Well, I'm going to show you exactly how to trigger BPA workflows directly from your Fiori application using API triggers.
Think of it like this: your user fills out a form in Fiori, hits submit, this enables communication between Fiori and BPA, allowing your workflow to start automatically based on form input.
 
What we're building today:
  1. A Fiori form that captures sales order data
  2. A BPA workflow that gets triggered automatically
  3. The connection between them using API triggers

An API trigger - It is a BPA allows external systems such as SAP Fiori, CPI, or any REST capable application to start a process by calling an API endpoint.

Part 1: Creating the BPA Automation with API Trigger

1. Go to BTP Lobby -> Create - > Automated Process -> Process.
You’ll see a new process canvas with an option to add a trigger.

2. Click API Trigger to set this as the starting point of your workflow.

3. Give a suitable name and click Create.

4. Add a Data Type that contains the fields you want to receive from Fiori.

5. Create a custom variable using the same Data Type created above.

6. Your workflow might look like this:

API Trigger
Condition (e.g., check if Net Amount > 1000)
-> If Yes ->  Auto Approval
-> If No -> User Approval
->  Acknowledgement Form or Rejection Form

Once ready, Save → Release → Deploy the process.

Note: After deployment only it will appear in Environment 

7. Go to Control Tower -> Environments -> Public (or your selected environment).
Click the three dots -> View.

8. Below is the sample JSON payload which it used to map values 

Copy this  you’ll use it in your Fiori app.

Part 2: Creating the Fiori App (Freestyle SAPUI5)

1. Open BAS -> File -> New Project from Template.

2. Select SAP Fiori generator and click start.

3. Select basic to create freestyle application and click next.

 

4. select Data Source and Service Selection

  • Data Source: Connect to a system

  • System: Choose your SAP system

  • Service: Select the OData service

Click Next.

5. Select yes for deployment configuration and click next.

6. Deployment configuration

  • Target -> Cloud Foundry

  • Destination Name -> Your destination

  • Add Router Module -> Managed Application Router

Finish the wizard.

7. In Cloud Foundry sign in and targets Choose SSO Passcode.
Click the link ->  Select Identity Provider -> Enter Passcode -> Sign in.

Click the URL, and it will take you to the Identity Provider selection page. Choose the default provider, verify that your user ID is correct, and continue. A passcode will be generated copy it, paste it and sign in.

8. simple Fiori form 

View1.view.xml

<mvc:View controllerName="salesorder.controller.View1"
    xmlns:mvc="sap.ui.core.mvc"
    xmlns="sap.m" xmlns:l="sap.ui.layout"
    xmlns:f="sap.ui.layout.form">

    <Page id="page" title="Sales Order Form">

        <VBox id="_IDGenVBox1" class="sapUiSmallMargin"   alignItems="Center">

            <!-- Centered container with limited width -->
            <VBox id="_IDGenVBox2" width="50%" class="sapUiSmallMarginAuto"  >

                <f:SimpleForm id="_IDGenSimpleForm"
                    editable="true"
                    layout="ResponsiveGridLayout"
                    labelSpanL="4" labelSpanM="4"
                    adjustLabelSpan="false"
                    emptySpanL="1" emptySpanM="1"
                    columnsL="1" columnsM="1">

                    <Label id="_IDGenLabel" text="Sales Order"/>
                    <Input id="_IDGenInput" value="{salesorderdetails/SalesOrder}" required="true"/>

                    <Label id="_IDGenLabel1" text="Sales Org"/>
                    <Input id="_IDGenInput1" value="{salesorderdetails/SalesOrg}" required="true"/>

                    <Label id="_IDGenLabel2" text="Distribution Channel"/>
                    <Input id="_IDGenInput2" value="{salesorderdetails/DistributionChannel}" required="true"/>

                    <Label id="_IDGenLabel3" text="Division"/>
                    <Input id="_IDGenInput3" value="{salesorderdetails/Division}" required="true"/>

                    <Label id="_IDGenLabel4" text="Document Date"/>
                    <DatePicker id="_IDGenDatePicker" value="{salesorderdetails/DocumentDate}" required="true"/>

                    <Label id="_IDGenLabel5" text="Created By"/>
                    <Input id="_IDGenInput4" value="{salesorderdetails/CreatedByUser}"/>

                    <Label id="_IDGenLabel6" text="Customer Number"/>
                    <Input id="_IDGenInput5" value="{salesorderdetails/CustomerNumber}" required="true"/>

                    <Label id="_IDGenLabel7" text="Order Amount"/>
                    <Input id="_IDGenInput6" value="{salesorderdetails/OrderAmount}" required="true"/>

                    <Label id="_IDGenLabel8" text="Purchase Order Number"/>
                    <Input id="_IDGenInput7" value="{salesorderdetails/PurchaseOrderNumber}" required="true"/>

                </f:SimpleForm>

                <!-- Submit button aligned to right -->
                <HBox id="_IDGenHBox" justifyContent="End" class="sapUiSmallMarginTop">
                    <Button id="_IDGenButton" text="Submit" type="Emphasized" press=".onSubmit"/>
                </HBox>

            </VBox>

        </VBox>

    </Page>

</mvc:View>

View1.controller.js

sap.ui.define([
    "sap/ui/core/mvc/Controller"
], (Controller) => {
    "use strict";

    return Controller.extend("salesorder.controller.View1", {
        onInit() {
        },
        
         _getExternalServiceRuntimeBaseURL: function () {
            var oComponent = sap.ui.core.Component.getOwnerComponentFor(this.getView());
                if (oComponent) {
                    var sAppId = oComponent.getManifestEntry("/sap.app/id");
                    var sAppPath = sAppId.replaceAll(".", "/");
                    var sAppModulePath = jQuery.sap.getModulePath(sAppPath);
                    return sAppModulePath;
                } else {
                    console.error("Component could not be found.");
                    return "";
                }
    
        },
        onSubmit() {
            var oView = this.getView();
            var url = "/sap_process_automation_service/workflow/rest/v1/workflow-instances";
            var sUrl = this._getExternalServiceRuntimeBaseURL() + url;
            try {
                var payload = {
                    "definitionId": "eu10.fin-dev-project.salesorder522.salesOrder",
                        "context": {
                        "salesorderdetails": {
                            "SalesOrder":  oView.byId("_IDGenInput").getValue(),
                                "SalesOrg": oView.byId("_IDGenInput1").getValue(),
                                    "DistributionChannel": oView.byId("_IDGenInput3").getValue(),
                                        "Division": oView.byId("_IDGenInput2").getValue(),
                                            "DocumentDate": String(oView.byId("_IDGenDatePicker").getValue() || ""),
                                                "CreatedByUser": oView.byId("_IDGenInput4").getValue(),
                                                    "CustomerNumber": String(oView.byId("_IDGenInput5").getValue()||""),
                                                        "OrderAmount": String(oView.byId("_IDGenInput6").getValue() || ""),
                                                            "PurchaseOrderNumber": String(oView.byId("_IDGenInput7").getValue() || "")
                        }
                    }
                }
                console.log("Payload: ", payload);
                  $.ajax({
                url: sUrl,
                type: "POST",
                dataType: "json",
                contentType: "application/json",
                data: JSON.stringify(payload),
                beforeSend: function (xhr) {
                    xhr.setRequestHeader("Accept", "application/json");
                },
                success: function (response) {
                    console.log("Success: ", response);
                }.bind(this),
                error: function (error) {
                    console.log("Error in POST call:", error);
                }

            });

            } catch (error) {
                console.error("Submission failed:", error);
            }
        }
    });
});

xs.app.json -

//add this in routes   
   {
      "source": "^/process_automation_service/(.*)$",
      "target": "$1",
      "destination": "process_automation_service",
      "authenticationType": "xsuaa",
      "csrfProtection": false
    }

9. Right-click mta.yaml -> Build MTA Project.
This generates an .mtar file in mta_archives.

10. Right-click the .mtar file -> Deploy MTA Archive.

11. Go to BTP cockpit -> HTML5 Applications -> click your app.

12. Fill the form -> Click Submit.
you will get success message in console

If it fails, check:

  • Destination configuration

  • xs-app.json routes

  • BPA credentials

13. Once triggered, the workflow reaches the approver in the BPA Inbox.

14. you will receive the acknowledge form once you received you can accept it.

15. Once everything is over you will see the completed status

16 Click the instance to see:

  • step details

  • submitted data

  • log

Note: In BPA, "Started By" usually shows the SAP user.
But when triggering through external systems (like Fiori), it may generate a system string instead of the real user ID.

So design your workflow logic accordingly.

Conclusion:  You've successfully built a seamless integration between SAP Fiori and SAP Build Process Automation.

What you accomplished:

  • Created an end-to-end automated workflow from Fiori form submission to BPA approval process
  • Implemented real-time API communication using REST triggers
  • Established a reusable integration pattern for future business processes

You're combining the user-friendly interface of Fiori with the powerful automation capabilities of BPA, while maintaining full connectivity to your core ABAP systems - creating a comprehensive end-to-end solution.

entry.title: SAP BTP Platform Technical User for CTMS Integration
entry.content:
SAP BTP Platform Technical User for CTMS Integration

 Introduction:

In SAP BTP environments integrated with SAP Identity Authentication Service (IAS), technical users are often required for

  • Platform automation
  • CI/CD integrations
  • Destination-based authentication
  • Cloud Foundry access
  • API consumption

This blog explains the step-by-step process to create and configure a BTP platform technical user authenticated via IAS and used with OAuth2Password authentication in destination configuration.

In this setup:

  • IAS acts as Custom Identity Provider
  • Technical user is created in IAS
  • User is added to BTP subaccounts (Org & Space level)
  • OAuth2Password flow is used for authentication
  • CTMS Destination is configured using CF login endpoint

Creating a Technical User in IAS tenant:

Login to IAS Admin Console:

  1. Go to Users
  2. Click Add User
  3. Maintain:
    • Username (e.g., CTMSADM)
    • Dummy Email ID (must be verified)
    • Strong password

Add Technical User to BTP Subaccounts

In SAP Business Technology Platform cockpit:

  1. Go to Global Account
  2. Navigate to Subaccount
  3. Choose Custom IAS IDP
  4. Add user to:
    • Org
    • Space
    • Required Role Collections

 

Add the IAS tech user in all subaccounts space and org by choosing the custom IDP IAS

After adding the users in all subaccounts

Longin to the below CF url with the technical user

https://login.cf.sa30.hana.ondemand.com/

Before configuring this user in destination make sure you that you able to login the above URL

Mention Origin key of your Custom IDP and login and it will take you to IAS sign in page

Successfully logged in

Now update the CTMS destination authentication to Oauth2Password

Maintain client ID as cf and secret should be empty

Token service url = https://login.cf.sa30.hana.ondemand.com/

Add the additional property origin and value should origin key of your platform user IDP

Now the destination for Deploy service will work as expected with Technical user created in IAS

Why Enable Technical User in IAS?

1) Secure Platform Automation

Allows:

  • Background jobs
  • API integrations
  • Deployment pipelines
  • Destination authentication

Without exposing human credentials.

2) Clear Separation of Duties

Separates:

  • Human Users
  • Service Accounts

This is critical for:

  • Audit compliance
  • SOX / ISO controls
  • Security governance

3) Controlled Access

You can:

  • Assign minimal role collections
  • Restrict space-level access
  • Monitor login activity

4) Centralized Identity Management

Since IAS is the trusted IDP:

  • All authentication flows through IAS
  • Password policies enforced centrally
  • User lifecycle managed in one place

Conclusion:

Enabling a dedicated technical user through SAP Identity Authentication Service for SAP Business Technology Platform integrations ensures a secure and scalable approach for automation scenarios. By separating service accounts from human users, organizations can enforce stronger security controls, simplify identity management, and maintain compliance with enterprise governance standards.

This approach is particularly useful for integration scenarios such as CI/CD pipelines, CTMS transport automation, API-based integrations, and destination authentication within Cloud Foundry environments. By implementing technical users with minimal required privileges and centralized authentication via IAS, administrators can ensure both operational efficiency and improved security posture across their BTP landscape.

entry.title: Event Mesh + AMQP: The Retry Trap Nobody Warns You About
entry.content:
Hello Experts,

I have seen a number of implementations of event-driven design involving SAP Event Mesh and the AMQP sender adapter on Cloud Integration. Most were built on the incorrect assumption that standard settings would be sufficient — without proper end-to-end testing to validate that assumption.

In this blog, I explain two patterns I have used in practice, along with their strengths and trade-offs. I have also added an important baseline section on why the native AMQP retry configuration alone is not enough.

Finally, we will look at whether Advanced Event Mesh requires the same workarounds — or whether it solves the problem out of the box.

Baseline: Why Native AMQP Retry Is Not Enough

The AMQP sender adapter does have a built-in Retry Details configuration (Max. Number of Retries + Delivery Status After Max. Retries). However, the native retry has a critical limitation: there is no configurable retry delay or backoff interval. The broker redelivers immediately and — if not set up correctly — indefinitely. This means a temporary downstream outage produces a burst of failures rather than graceful back-pressure.

Max Redelivery Count in Event Mesh queue configuration set as as EMPTY/0 or Max. Number of Retries in the AMQP sender adapter set as EMPTY results in indefinite, immediate redeliveries that flood both the EM queue and the CI tenant — a serious stability risk.

📝Remark: According to SAP Note 3269414, configuring a Dead Message Queue (DMQ) in Event Broker can help limit retries, but this is not affecting Event Mesh.

The practical hint: to effectively limit number of retries you need to set retry number > 0 in Event Mesh queue settings and AMQP adapter.

JMSXDeliveryCount header is auto-incremented only for internal AMQP retries, not overall number of retries (AMQP + Broker side). JMSRedelivered header switch from false to true. Therefore retry strategy cannot be designed inside Iflow similary to JMS (SAPJMSRetries header). A potential workaround is to use a custom header (please see the description in Option 2).

As you can see, we can limit the number of retries, but we can't set a solid retry interval. That's exactly why I wrote this blog post with two solutions.

Each pattern has its strengths and trade-offs. Let's dive in.

Option 1: Decouple AMQP from iFlow Logic via JMS

In my previous blog series on JMS, I compared whether to use JMS or an Event Broker directly:
JMS Mastery Series Part 3 — JMS in Production

Actually, both approaches can work in synergy for certain scenarios — retry handling being a prime example.

The core idea: use the AMQP adapter only to ingest the message from Event Mesh, immediately write it to a JMS queue, and let the JMS adapter handle all retry logic from that point forward. Event Mesh is decoupled from the processing logic as soon as the message lands in JMS. The iFlow finishes with COMPLETED status and the message is acknowledged and removed from the EM queue.

How it works

  • The AMQP sender adapter receives the message from Event Mesh.
  • The iFlow immediately writes the payload to a JMS queue, which acts as an internal buffer.
  • A second iFlow reads from JMS and executes the full processing logic.
  • JMS handles retries natively with a configurable interval and exponential backoff.

Pros

  • The Event Mesh connection is short-lived — released immediately after the JMS write, freeing EM resources.
  • Full JMS retry capability: configurable retry interval, exponential backoff, and maximum retry interval — none of which are available in the AMQP adapter.
  • Proven pattern — behaves identically to any JMS-backed HTTP or SOAP integration.

Cons

  • Consumes additional JMS resources — two queues are involved: the external EM queue and the internal JMS queue.
  • Increased operational overhead: more moving parts to monitor and maintain.

To reduce the number of JMS queues, you can assign one JMS queue per group of interfaces, or even one JMS queue for all interfaces from a single Event Mesh instance. However, this breaks the 1:1 mapping between EM queues and JMS queues, with important consequences.

💡Personal note: Even as a JMS advocate, I apply this pattern selectively. It is not the default. I add JMS on top of AMQP only when the iFlow logic is genuinely heavy — complex mappings, multi-step orchestration with high processing time or calls to multiple external systems (Recipient List pattern). Second use case is when you truly need robust retry control with exponential backoff. For operationally lightweight flows, Option 2 is cleaner.

Option 2: Retry Queue + TTL + DMQ (No JMS)

This pattern implements a retry-with-delay mechanism entirely within Event Mesh, using a dedicated Retry queue and TTL expiration to simulate retry backoff — without involving JMS at all.

How it works

  • The main iFlow catches errors in the exception subprocess.
  • If the error is transient and eligible for retry, the message is forwarded to a dedicated Retry queue in Event Mesh via an AMQP receiver adapter.
  • The Retry queue is configured with: DMQ = original processing queue, Max TTL = desired retry delay in seconds.
  • When the TTL expires, Event Mesh automatically moves the message to the DMQ — which is the original queue — and processing restarts.
  • The iFlow ends with an End Message event (COMPLETED status), so no automatic AMQP retry is triggered.

The TTL value effectively becomes your retry delay interval. You can implement increasing delays by chaining multiple retry queues with different TTL values, though this adds configuration complexity.

This creates a controlled retry loop: the message cycles between the Retry queue and the original queue until it either succeeds or the retry counter threshold is reached.

Tracking retry count

Because JMSXDeliveryCount resets to 1 and JMSRedelivered resets to false on every queue hop — this is a new delivery, not a broker redelivery — you cannot rely on these headers to count retry attempts.

Two approaches for maintaining a retry counter:

  • Custom header approach: add a RetryCount header to the message before forwarding it to the Retry queue, increment it on each pass, and stop retrying when it exceeds your threshold. This avoids any persistence layer and is the preferred approach for most scenarios.
  • Data Store approach: persist a counter keyed on a message correlation ID. This can introduce read/write lock contention at high throughput.

Pros

  • No JMS resources consumed.
  • Retry delay is cleanly controlled via TTL — no thread blocking.

Cons

  • The setup is non-obvious and requires careful EM queue configuration.
  • JMSXDeliveryCount and JMSRedelivered are unreliable for retry counting — custom counter logic is required.
  • A Data Store-based counter introduces contention risk at high throughput.
  • No native exponential backoff — fixed TTL only, unless you chain queues.

💡Personal note: This is my preferred option. Clean, JMS-free, and elegant once you understand the TTL mechanics.

Option 3 (Not Recommended): Groovy Sleep as Retry Delay

This is a workaround I have seen in the field: using a sleep() call inside a Groovy script to introduce a delay before retrying, relying on the AMQP adapter's built-in retry to re-execute the iFlow.

Pros

  • No additional infrastructure — no JMS, no Retry queue, no TTL configuration.
  • Simple to implement for a quick proof of concept.

Cons

  • The AMQP connection to Event Mesh remains open and locked for the entire sleep duration, preventing EM from reclaiming the resource.
  • The CI worker thread is blocked for the sleep duration. Under concurrent load, this exhausts the worker thread pool and degrades performance for all iFlows on the same tenant — not just this one.
  • sleep() in Groovy on Cloud Integration is a tenant-level anti-pattern. At scale, it can cause cascading timeouts across unrelated integrations. This is not merely an aesthetic concern — it is a production stability risk.
  • No retry limit control without additional explicit design.

💡Personal note: Even though it is simple to implement, never use this in production. It is a quick hack, but the connection lock makes it an anti-pattern for any scenario requiring scalability. I would not recommend it to anyone.

Pattern Comparison

Use this table to guide your pattern selection:

CriteriaOption 1: JMS BridgeOption 2: TTL + DMQOption 3: Groovy SleepAMQP Retry
JMS RequiredYesNoNoNo
Retry Delay Control✅Full — retry interval, exponential backoff, max retry interval✅Via TTL — retry interval only, no exponential backoff⚠️Via sleep command only❌Immediate redelivery only
Retry CounterSAPJMSRetries headerCustom header / Data StoreEM + AMQP settings or Custom header / Data StoreEM + AMQP settings or Custom header / Data Store
EM Connection DurationShort - decoupled from iFlow processingStandard - but prevents long-lived connections caused by retriesLong ⚠️Standard
High Throughput FitYes (with care)Limited if Data Store used as counterNoYes (with care)
Setup ComplexityMediumMediumLowLow
Recommended ForHeavy iFlow logic, strict retry needsMost event-driven cases — low-to-moderate iFlow logic, flat retry interval is sufficientNot recommended — development/POC use only at mostSimple flows only — needs redesign for production

Does Retrying in Advanced Event Mesh Work Out of the Box?

Short answer: yes — AEM retry logic works like a charm. This is not surprising given that Advanced Event Mesh is not an entirely new product, but rather a white-label of Solace PubSub+, a very mature messaging solution with years of production use behind it.

What Can We Expect from AEM and the AEM Sender Adapter in Cloud Integration?

The full spectrum of retry configuration is available directly at the adapter level:

  • Retry interval
  • Maximum retry interval
  • Exponential backoff multiplier
  • Maximum number of retries

All of the above are configured directly on the AEM adapter in Cloud Integration, and all work as expected. No surprises, no disappointments. It is the best of both worlds:

  • JMS-level retry interval configuration is available
  • Event Mesh-level retry limit configuration is available

AEM offers not only adapter-level retries (CI side) but also broker-level retries (Max Redelivery Count on the queue). After CI-side retries are exhausted, a failed status is sent back to AEM, which then redelivers the message according to its own Max Redelivery Count — so the total retry attempts is a product of both settings, not a simple sum. Factor this in when configuring both to avoid unexpectedly high total retry counts.
This works like described above if Acknowledgment mode is set to: Automatic On Exchange Completed and Settlement outcome: Failed.

If you are fortunate enough to be working with AEM on your project, you do not need to apply any of the workarounds described above or add JMS on top of your event-driven design purely for the sake of working retry logic.

Final Thoughts

These two patterns exist because the AMQP adapter's native retry has no configurable delay. Until SAP adds native retry backoff to the AMQP adapter — or Event Mesh provides broker-side delay queues as a first-class feature — these workarounds remain necessary for production-grade event-driven designs.

My default recommendations:

  • Start with Option 2 (TTL + DMQ) for most flows. It is clean, JMS-free, and operationally lightweight.
  • Escalate to Option 1 (JMS bridge) only when the iFlow processing logic is genuinely complex or you need exponential backoff.
  • Never use the Groovy Sleep approach in production.
  • If AEM is available, the standard adapter configuration gives you a complete, production-grade solution out of the box — no workarounds required.

Additional Notes for Production

Idempotency and duplicate handling: Complement your chosen retry pattern with idempotency handling to avoid duplicate processing in the target system. The best place to implement deduplication is the receiver application, not the middleware. Also note that the event broker does not guarantee exactly-once processing — you must design for duplicates and assume at-least-once delivery.

Define the final stop after retries: Always define what happens when retries are exhausted. Route the message to a Dead Message Queue (DMQ) or equivalent quarantine mechanism, and ensure there is an operational process for monitoring and reprocessing those messages.

Are you using any of these patterns? Have you found other approaches? Comments, questions, and observations are very welcome.

entry.title: Re: Questions : March 2026 Developer Challenge - Joule for Developers in ABAP
entry.content:
Hi,
While completing Week 1 I faced an error,
In Execise 3.4 we are making fields read only,

But again in 4.5, we are updating those fields, if I kept in Local mode it is going to dump as it is not inside RAP Framework, Did I miss anything, For now I have removed readonly for these fields.

entry.title: Re: BAPI_SALESORDER_CHANGE returns success message but do...
entry.content: @bigflatfoot while that is required it should be noted that a COMMIT should NEVER be issued directly inside processing of a user-exit.  You may have an exception if it's a different document, but it would still be more appropriate to call a separate wrapper function in a new unit of work.

entry.title: Collection Management BFC - Payment promise with SEPA direct debit
entry.content:
Hello everyone,
We have set up the Scope Item BFC Collection Management in our company. Our customers want to use payment promises with the corresponding instalment plan.
Now a question has arisen: if a business partner is created in their master record with SEPA direct debit, how does this work with the instalment plan?

Example:
A customer has an invoice for £300 and wants to pay it in instalments of £100 per month. We create an instalment plan in the payment promise. Since they have SEPA direct debit, the money is automatically debited from their account. 
How can we set it up so that only the first instalment of the invoice is debited, i.e. £100 and not £300?

Can you please send me instructions or a solution? Or is it even possible?

Thank you in advance.
Best regards,

Nathalie

entry.title: SAP Fiori Elements: New Documentation Structure
entry.content:
We're excited to announce a complete restructure of the SAP Fiori elements documentation. We now provide separate entry points for OData V4 and OData V2, making it easier to navigate their different feature sets. The new structure reflects our focus on SAP Fiori elements for OData V4 and its building blocks, and it is aligned with the SAP Fiori development portal to ensure a consistent user experience.

Separation of OData V4 and V2 Documentation

Separated entry points for OData V4 and OData V2 make it easier to find your way around our many features. After choosing the right entry point, you can be sure that all the information you see is relevant to the version you use.

For OData V4, the revised documentation structure is centered around our building blocks. All related features are now placed together, reducing browsing time and helping you find what you need faster.

For OData V2, we've kept the documentation structure unchanged to ensure stability for our customers. You'll still find all topics exactly where you expect them. And while our focus going forward is on V4, we continue to support and update the V2 documentation as well.

Focus on Building Blocks at the Core

The new documentation structure organizes topics around building blocks, which are the foundation of both floorplans and custom pages.

You may notice that some features can be consumed in different ways depending on your context:

  • When a building block is used within a standard floorplan, you typically configure its features through annotations or manifest settings.
  • When the same building block is used on a custom page, you control its features through the properties and methods it exposes.

Our documentation highlights these differences with clear notes, helping you choose the right approach for your use case.

Harmonization Between Documentation & SAP Fiori Development Portal 

The SAP Fiori development portal is our one-stop resource designed to help all project team members – from developers to business analysts – explore options, collaborate, and make informed decisions when building SAP Fiori apps. It supports the metadata-driven approach of SAP Fiori elements for OData V4, which offers two flexible paths:

  • Custom layouts using reusable building blocks, or
  • Standard floorplans for rapid development.

Both paths deliver consistent, enterprise-ready applications that follow the SAP Design System guidelines, ensuring harmonized user experience while allowing for configuration when needed.

You can immediately see the harmonization between the structure of the SAP Fiori development portal and our new documentation structure:

Ongoing Enhancements to Content

We’re continuously enhancing our documentation – not only with new features, but also by enriching the content with cross-references, linking related topics, and connecting you to relevant resources.

We also listen to your feedback, so please continue to provide us with ideas about how to further improve our content!

entry.title: Manage Sales Order V2 Extension
entry.content:
Hello,

I’m working on a project where we are replacing a Web Dynpro–based customer portal with a new Fiori launchpad. As part of this redesign, we need to ensure customers only see data relevant to their customer number across multiple standard Fiori applications.

Currently developing in S4 Private Cloud 2023 FPS02.

For several V2 apps(Odata version 2 - SEGW project), I’ve been able to achieve this by creating a BAS extension project and calling an exposed CDS API to default the Sold‑To Party based on the customer associated with the logged‑in user. This approach worked in most V2 apps.

However, I have not been able to achieve the same for the Manage Sales Orders V2 (F3893 / F1873A successor) app. I’ve attempted:

  • BAS adaptation project extensions
  • Behavior extensions
  • Metadata extensions
  • DCL‑based restrictions

…but none of these approaches successfully default or enforce the Sold‑To Party for this particular app.

Do you have any guidance, supported patterns, or recommended approaches for defaulting the Sold‑To Party (or restricting visible data) for the Manage Sales Order V2 app specifically?

Our goal is to allow each customer to see and work only with their own sales orders when using the standard SAP app, as part of a clean‑core customer portal. Essentially restrict the application by Sold-To Party. The user's are assigned their Sold-To Party by a customer table which is maintained by a business super user. 

Any insights or recommended architectures would be greatly appreciated.

Jared.

Tag: @CE6

entry.title: Re: Compensation Information View in Latest People Profile
entry.content: I am a bit confused on what you are asking. Are you referring to displaying the monthly rate in EC (compensation Portlet) or displaying the monthly rate in the form. The leading practice would be to have a pay component specifically for the base rate and then have the annualized salary as a pay component group. This is useful when the company has employees paid at different intervals. Once you do that, the compensation details are displayed and the latest people profile will also display the information. Configuration of the latest people profile is not the issue as it just links to the cards vs actually defining the content of what exists in the cards.

entry.title: Re: Impacts of Business Rules modifying Effective Start Date and Suggestions to SAP Team
entry.content: Hi @muralidharansrinivasan my previous comment wasn't clear—the date (past or future) doesn't matter. If pre-existing data corruption issues are present, they can still result in these errors and must be cleaned manually. This should be rare, and once cleaned, the issue should not recur. No patch can fix existing corrupted data; customers experiencing this must raise a support ticket for manual intervention.

entry.title: Re: How to calculate bonus proration in Variable Pay using a lookup table?
entry.content: I don't think this is the optimum way to do this. Is this an EC client? If so, the way to do this would be to create a rule for employee history that excludes the records for when the employee is on leave. This make the employee ineligible while on leave and the system would prorate accordingly. For example, the employee is active 1/1 to 6/30 and then on leave from 7/1 to 9/30 then active again from 10/1 to 12/31. The result would be bringing over records 1 and 3, and the proration will occur dynamically. If not EC, when you import the history file, just exclude records for when employees are on leave.

entry.title: Re: Proactive Cross Plant Planning in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
entry.content: Hi Tim, The proactive maintenance activities are planned always by the centralized plant, but Ad-hoc planning for reactive maintenance activities are planned by the same plant that performs the tasks. E.g. Proactive Maintenance Plan PlPl 1001 = Plant 1003. Reactive Maintenance PlPl 1003 = Plant 1003.

entry.title: SAP Generative AI Hub: A Practitioner's Setup Guide
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY  |  PRACTICAL GUIDE  |  SAP BTP  |  SAP AI CORE

SAP Generative AI Hub: A Practitioner's Setup Guide

Step-by-step configuration from BTP entitlements to your first foundation model API call — including the friction points that aren't in the official documentation.

By Deepa Dorairaj   |  SAP AI Solution Architect  |  SAP BTP & AI Core  |  Published 2026

TL;DR: SAP Generative AI Hub gives you governed, auditable access to GPT-4o, Claude, Gemini, Mistral, and SAP's own models from a single BTP service. Getting there requires navigating BTP entitlements, AI Core service instance setup, resource group configuration, and API authentication — none of which is as straightforward as the documentation suggests. This guide covers the complete setup path and the specific friction points that will slow you down if you don't know about them.

What the Generative AI Hub Actually Is

SAP's Generative AI Hub is the access layer within SAP AI Core that gives you a single, governed interface to multiple foundation models — GPT-4o via Azure OpenAI, Claude from Anthropic, Gemini from Google, Mistral, SAP-RPT-1, and others — without building separate integrations to each provider. It handles authentication, usage tracking, rate limiting, and compliance logging in a way that meets enterprise governance requirements.

For SAP architects and data engineers, the key value proposition is straightforward: instead of managing separate API keys, separate billing accounts, and separate compliance considerations for each AI provider, the Generative AI Hub abstracts all of that into a single BTP service with consistent access patterns, audit trails, and cost visibility.

What the documentation does not prepare you for is how much configuration stands between you and your first working API call. This guide covers every step — including the ones that aren't clearly documented — so you can get from zero to a working foundation model integration without the friction we went through.

BEFORE YOU START 

Prerequisites and What You Actually Need

Before beginning setup, confirm you have the following. Missing any of these is the most common reason setup stalls before it starts:

  • A BTP global account with available entitlements — a trial account works for initial experimentation but has model and quota limitations
  • A BTP subaccount in a supported region — AI Core availability varies by region and not all models are available in all regions (covered in detail below)
  • Space Manager or Organization Manager role in your BTP subaccount — standard Developer roles are insufficient for service instance creation
  • Access to the BTP Cockpit — your entry point for all entitlement and service configuration

Region matters more than you might expect. GPT-4o, Claude, and Gemini availability through the Generative AI Hub is not uniform across all BTP regions. US East, EU West, and AP regions have the broadest model availability. If you set up AI Core in an unsupported region, you will successfully create the service instance but find that certain models are simply not visible in the Hub. Check SAP's current model availability matrix before choosing your subaccount region.

STEP 1 OF 5 

BTP Entitlement Setup

Adding the AI Core Entitlement

Navigate to your BTP global account and open the Entitlements section. This is where most teams hit their first friction point — the AI Core service and the Generative AI Hub are separate entitlements and both need to be explicitly added before you can create a service instance.

In the BTP Cockpit, go to Entitlements → Entity Assignments → select your subaccount → Configure Entitlements → Add Service Plans.

Search for and add the following:

  • SAP AI Core — select the Extended service plan (not the Free tier, which has significant model and quota restrictions)
  • SAP AI Launchpad — needed to access the Generative AI Hub UI; easy to miss if you assume the Hub is part of AI Core itself

The most common entitlement mistake: adding SAP AI Core but not SAP AI Launchpad. AI Core is the runtime service. AI Launchpad is the UI that gives you access to the Generative AI Hub prompt editor and model catalog. Without AI Launchpad, you can make API calls but you cannot use the visual interface for prompt experimentation. Add both entitlements before proceeding.

Quota Allocation

After adding the entitlements, allocate quota to your subaccount. The default quota allocation for AI Core is zero — you must explicitly set it. In the Entitlements screen, set the quota for the Extended plan to at least 1 unit to enable service instance creation.

Quota and billing transparency is a genuine friction point here. The Generative AI Hub bills on a token consumption basis per model, but the BTP entitlement setup does not show you projected costs or usage limits upfront. Before running any workloads in production, contact your SAP account team to understand the cost model for your specific contract — trial and CPEA accounts have different billing mechanics.

 

Step

Action

Detail

1

Open BTP Cockpit

Navigate to your global account → Subaccounts → select your subaccount

2

Open Entitlements

Entitlements → Entity Assignments → Configure Entitlements

3

Add AI Core

Add Service Plans → search 'SAP AI Core' → select Extended plan

4

Add AI Launchpad

Add Service Plans → search 'SAP AI Launchpad' → select Standard plan

5

Set quota

Set AI Core Extended plan quota to minimum 1 unit → Save

STEP 2 OF 5 

Creating the AI Core Service Instance

Service Instance Creation

With entitlements in place, navigate to your BTP subaccount → Services → Service Marketplace → search for SAP AI Core → select the Extended plan → Create.

During instance creation you will be prompted to provide a JSON configuration parameter. For standard Generative AI Hub access, the default configuration is sufficient — do not skip this step by leaving it empty, as certain model access features require the configuration to be present even if it is set to default values.

{

  "serviceName": "aicore",

  "description": "AI Core instance for Generative AI Hub access"

}

Creating a Service Key

After the service instance is created, you need a service key to generate the credentials your applications will use for API authentication. In the service instance detail view, select Service Keys → Create → provide a name → Create.

The service key JSON contains the clientid, clientsecret, url (for OAuth token endpoint), and serviceurls.AI_API_URL (your AI Core API base URL). Save this immediately and store it securely — you will need all four values for API authentication.

API authentication is more complex than a simple API key. AI Core uses OAuth 2.0 client credentials flow. Your application must first call the token endpoint with the clientid and clientsecret to obtain a bearer token, then include that token in each AI Core API call. Tokens expire and must be refreshed. Many teams expect a simple API key pattern and are surprised by the OAuth requirement. Plan your authentication implementation before writing any AI logic.

STEP 3 OF 5 

Resource Group Configuration

What Resource Groups Are and Why They Matter

A resource group in SAP AI Core is a logical isolation boundary for AI workloads — models, deployments, and executions run within a resource group context. It is not obvious from the documentation that resource group configuration is a prerequisite for accessing the Generative AI Hub, but it is. Without a configured resource group, model deployments will not be visible even after AI Core and AI Launchpad are correctly set up.

Resource group configuration is done through the AI Core API, not through the BTP Cockpit UI — which is another friction point teams don't anticipate. You will need to make a direct API call to create your resource group using the service key credentials you obtained in Step 2.

Creating the Resource Group via API

First, obtain an OAuth token using your service key credentials:

# Step 1: Get OAuth token

curl -X POST '<token_url>/oauth/token' \

  -H 'Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded' \

  -d 'grant_type=client_credentials' \

  -d 'client_id=<clientid>' \

  -d 'client_secret=<clientsecret>'

 

Then create your resource group using the bearer token:

# Step 2: Create resource group

curl -X PUT '<AI_API_URL>/v2/admin/resourceGroups' \

  -H 'Authorization: Bearer <token>' \

  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \

  -d '{"resourceGroupId": "default"}'

Once the resource group is created, return to the AI Launchpad. You should now see the resource group available in the dropdown. Select it and navigate to the Generative AI Hub — the model catalog will now be visible and accessible.

STEP 4 OF 5 

Accessing the Generative AI Hub and Available Models

Navigating to the Hub

With AI Launchpad subscribed and your resource group configured, navigate to your BTP subaccount → Instances and Subscriptions → SAP AI Launchpad → Go to Application. Select your AI Core connection, choose your resource group, and open the Generative AI Hub from the left navigation.

The Hub has two primary sections you will use immediately: the Chat interface for interactive prompt testing and the Prompt Editor for structured experimentation with system prompts, parameters, and response comparison across models.

Available Foundation Models

The following models were available and tested during our setup. Model availability evolves — check the SAP AI Core release notes for the current catalog as new models are added regularly:

 

Model

Provider

Type

Best For

GPT-4o

Azure OpenAI

General LLM

Complex reasoning, code generation, document analysis

GPT-4o mini

Azure OpenAI

General LLM

Cost-efficient tasks, high-volume processing

Claude 3.5 Sonnet

Anthropic

General LLM

Long context, nuanced analysis, structured output

Claude 3 Haiku

Anthropic

General LLM

Fast, cost-efficient, high-volume classification

Gemini 1.5 Pro

Google

General LLM

Multimodal, very long context window

Mistral Large

Mistral AI

General LLM

European data residency, strong multilingual

Mistral Small

Mistral AI

General LLM

Cost-efficient European-hosted option

SAP-RPT-1

SAP

Tabular/structured

Numerical predictions on SAP tabular data

Not all models are available in all regions. If a model you expect to see is missing from your Hub catalog, the most likely cause is that your AI Core instance is in a region where that model is not yet deployed. This is not always clearly surfaced in the UI — the model simply does not appear rather than showing as unavailable. Check SAP's regional availability documentation if your expected models are missing.

STEP 5 OF 5 

Making Your First API Call

Using the Prompt Editor for Experimentation

Before writing any code, use the Generative AI Hub's built-in Prompt Editor to verify your setup is working and to experiment with models. The Prompt Editor allows you to set a system prompt, write a user message, adjust temperature and max token parameters, and compare responses across multiple models side by side.

This is the fastest way to validate that your entitlements, service instance, resource group, and model access are all correctly configured before investing time in application code.

Direct API Integration

For application integration, the AI Core API follows a consistent pattern across all models. After obtaining an OAuth token, construct your request to the chat completions endpoint:

import requests

# Step 1: Get OAuth bearer token

token_response = requests.post(

    f'{token_url}/oauth/token',

    data={

        'grant_type': 'client_credentials',

        'client_id': client_id,

        'client_secret': client_secret

    }

)

bearer_token = token_response.json()['access_token']

# Step 2: Call foundation model via AI Core API

headers = {

    'Authorization': f'Bearer {bearer_token}',

    'Content-Type': 'application/json',

    'AI-Resource-Group': 'default'

}

 

payload = {

    'model': 'gpt-4o',

    'messages': [

        {'role': 'system', 'content': 'You are an SAP data analyst.'},

        {'role': 'user', 'content': 'Summarize this SAP exception log: ...'}

    ],

    'max_tokens': 500,

    'temperature': 0.3

}

 

response = requests.post(

    f'{ai_api_url}/v2/inference/deployments/<deployment_id>/chat/completions',

    headers=headers,

    json=payload

)

print(response.json()['choices'][0]['message']['content'])

The deployment_id in the API endpoint URL is model-specific and changes when SAP updates model versions. Retrieve current deployment IDs programmatically from the AI Core API rather than hardcoding them — hardcoded deployment IDs are the most common cause of sudden API failures after a model version update.

Switching Between Models

The same API pattern works across all models in the Generative AI Hub — only the model parameter and deployment_id change. This is the core value of the Hub's abstraction layer: your application code does not need to change when you switch from GPT-4o to Claude or Mistral. The authentication, request structure, and response format are consistent.

The Friction Points: A Summary

For teams setting up the Generative AI Hub for the first time, these are the specific issues that will slow you down if you don't know about them upfront:

 

Step

Action

Detail

1

Missing AI Launchpad entitlement

AI Core alone is not sufficient — add SAP AI Launchpad separately or you will have no UI access to the Hub

2

Zero default quota

BTP allocates zero quota by default — explicitly set quota to at least 1 unit after adding the entitlement

3

Resource group not created

Models are invisible in the Hub until a resource group exists — create via API call before opening the Launchpad

4

OAuth not simple API key

AI Core uses OAuth 2.0 client credentials — implement token refresh logic before writing AI application code

5

Regional model gaps

Models missing from your catalog are likely unavailable in your region — check availability before choosing subaccount region

6

Hardcoded deployment IDs

Deployment IDs change with model updates — retrieve programmatically to avoid silent failures after SAP updates

What You Can Build From Here

With the Generative AI Hub correctly configured, the path to production SAP AI applications becomes significantly clearer. The governed, multi-model access layer means your team can experiment across GPT-4o, Claude, Gemini, and Mistral without managing separate provider relationships, and switch models based on performance, cost, or compliance requirements without rewriting application code.

The natural next steps from this setup are connecting the Generative AI Hub to your HANA Cloud vector store for RAG-based applications on SAP data, building SAP Joule extensions that leverage foundation model capabilities, and integrating AI Core into your BTP CAP application workflows for intelligent enterprise applications.

The setup friction is real but it is a one-time cost. Once the entitlements, service instance, resource group, and authentication are in place, the development experience is significantly smoother than managing individual AI provider integrations.

If you hit any of the friction points listed above or encounter issues not covered here, drop a comment below. These are the setups where community knowledge compounds quickly — your specific error message or configuration issue is likely something another practitioner has already solved.

An SAP Inside Track is a free, community-driven event open to everyone — customers, consultants, freelancers, developers, architects, and business professionals alike. entry.title: Mastering Data Loads in SAP Sales & Service Cloud V2 - Proven Strategies for Accuracy & Performance
entry.content:
In this blog post, let’s discuss the best practices and recommendations to be followed while performing data loads into SAP Sales & Service Cloud Version 2. Data loading is one of the most critical activities in any implementation or migration project—whether you are onboarding master data, preparing for a cutover, migrating from V1, or integrating with SAP S/4HANA. A well-executed data load ensures a clean, reliable, and scalable foundation for all downstream business processes. On the other hand, poorly sequenced loads, missing External IDs, misconfigured rate limits, or active automations can cause message failures, performance degradation, and even business downtime, as seen in several real-world cases across customers. For this reason, following a structured, proven approach is essential to ensure consistency, avoid rework, and guarantee a smooth go‑live.


Recommendations for Data Loads via REST API calls

  • Rate limit is applicable for this type of data loads. Check the section Rate Limit to understand more
  • Deactivate the Autoflows for the entities you load the data
  • Deactivate the pre/post hooks if not necessary
  • Disable standard events for the data loading entity and related entities.
  • Deactivate the outbound integration to SAP S4HANA or ERP by disabling the corresponding communication system
  • Ensure you use an API user for these types of calls – Using API user will have lot of benefits in terms of performance and helps in faster data loads.

Note: Using Business User for this data loading is prohibited as it may cause performance problems and delays.


Recommendations for Data Loads via Data Impex tool (Import Create or Import Update)

  • Rate limit is NOT applicable for this type of data load
  • Deactivate the outbound integration by disabling the communication system and/or communication configuration, so the data is not sent to connected S4HANA or ERP systems
  • Deactivate the autoflows that may trigger events to external systems
  • Disable the standard events for the data loading entity and related entities
  • Deactivate the pre/post hooks if not necessary. Using pre/post hooks during the data loads will lead to higher processing times.


Recommendations for Data Loads using Pre-Packaged Integration (S4HANA or ERP)

  • Rate limit is applicable. Check below for more details
  • Deactivate the autoflows for the entities you load the data
  • Deactivate the pre/post hooks if not necessary. Using pre/post hooks during the data loads will lead to higher processing times.
  • Disable the standard events for the data loading entity and related entities

Master Data Loads from S4HANA or ERP

  • Recommended packet size is between 50 to 200 for high volume objects. Ensure you follow the below sequence
  • Code lists by using Asynchronous and scheduler-based communication
  • Then load the Organizational Data – Sales Orgs, Sales Offices, Sales Groups
  • Then start loading Business Partners, Account hierarchies, Employees, Products, Product Hierarchies, Registered products, Installed Base, Installation Points, Exchange Rates
  • After all master data is completed, then start loading transactional data such as Sales Quotes / Sales Orders / Service documents / Contracts etc
  • More detailed blog on Data Loading Best Practises from SAP S4HANA / S4HANA Cloud / ERP to Sales & Service Cloud V2 will be released soon.  Watch this space!! 

 

RATE LIMIT

What is Rate Limit

A rate limit is the maximum number of API calls a tenant, or user is allowed to make within a fixed period. It is enforced automatically by the platform to protect system stability and ensure fair usage

Why is Rate Limit Important

Rate limits are introduced to protect platform stability and regulate high volume data loads. Having rate limit can keep multi-tenant environments fair in shared infrastructure.

What type of calls are considered under rate limits

The calls from external systems – while consuming the REST APIs for third party integration or the calls from SAP S4HANA / ERP using prepackaged integration are currently RATE LIMITED.

What type of calls are not considered under rate limit

The calls from your connected groupware applications, mobile applications, V1 to V2 migration using Data Transition Tool are not Rate Limited

How to Calculate Rate Limit for your tenant

If you are loading the data into test tenants – 30000 API calls per day are allowed. For production tenants - “150000 + (No of licensed users * 1500)”  are allowed. After the limit is exhausted, the calls fail in the immediate source system (external application or SAP CPI) with HTTP 429 error messages.

How to monitor the API Usage

You can monitor the API Usage during data loading process – check the below link.
https://help.sap.com/docs/CX_NG_SVC/56436b4e8fa84dc8b4408c7795a012c4/07a150dce5534e6095d09c1e79430f41.html

As mentioned in the document, you can use your connected or embedded SAC to build a story based on the data source “Aggregated API Statistics” which updates the API usage, identify trends, and ensure fair use of resources to prevent overload and abuse

Requesting Temporary Rate Limit Enhancement

To increase the rate limit for certain number of days – create an incident from launchpad to CEC-CRM-CO-OPS with following information (Mandatory)

  • Either chose the correct tenant while creating the incident or mention the tenant URL
  • Approximate Data Volume in total (schedule of data loads each day)
  • Start Date & End Date of Data Loads
  • What are the different entities for which the data is loaded? Like Leads / Accounts / Opportunities / Sales Orders / Registered Products etc
  • API user details (REST API calls)

entry.title: My purchase requisition partial cancellation confirmation error
entry.content:
Logged in French

In "My purchase Requisition", a confirmation can be done. Usually it is possible to cancel/return the confirmation already done (respecting the status of the PR item).

Functionnal Steps

1. Logged in French language
2. Create a Purchase Requisition of 10 unit quantity : 'Pce'
3. Once approved, create the Purchase Order
4. Once done, confirm the Purchase Requisition item of 10Pce
5. In "my Purchase Requisition" , select the concerned purchase requisition, click on 'Return'
6. In the step of return, try to return a partial quantity, for example quantity of 6Pce

---> error

entry.title: Partner learning accelerator: Solution architect - SAP Business Technology Platform
entry.content:
Every great solution architecture usually starts the same way: a messy whiteboard, a few big business goals, and the question… how do we design this the right way?

If you’re working with SAP Business Technology Platform, you’ve probably been there.

That’s why we’re hosting the Partner learning accelerator: Solution architect – SAP BTP, a 90-minute live session designed for partners preparing for the SAP BTP Solution Architect certification.

We’ll break down what strong architecture really looks like across SAP BTP.  Not just theory. Real considerations: how to translate business requirements into technical blueprints, how integration strategies fit into enterprise landscapes, and where governance, DevOps, analytics, and master data play their role.

The goal is simple: help experienced partners connect the architectural dots faster and approach solution design with more clarity and confidence.

If you’re already building on SAP BTP and thinking about the next step in your architect journey… this session will be time well spent.

Register now and join the conversation.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2025/nov/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00012265.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPEcosystem #EnterpriseArchitecture #CloudStrategy #PartnerEnablement

entry.title: Removing HTTP headers using PI web dispatcher
entry.content:
Hello everyone:

I have a client connecting to my PI server and sending the following HTTP headers, which apparently aren't accepted by my SOAP web service:

* Upgrade: h2c
* Connection: Upgrade, HTTP2-Settings

I'd like to know if anyone has encountered a similar situation and how they resolved it.

I've considered using the web dispatcher to suppress the headers that PI doesn't allow, which prevent the messages from even reaching the SOAP communication channel, but I don't know how to do it.

Thanks in advance for any help. Best regards.

entry.title: CreateTest User ID for S/4HANA Public cloud
entry.content:
Hello SAP Support Team,

We are currently preparing for the Functional Integration Testing (FIT) phase in our SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Test Tenant and would like guidance on the recommended approach for setting up mass test users for manual testing.

Our requirement is to create multiple test user IDs in the test system and assign each of our custom business roles to dedicated test users. The objective is to test every custom role individually during the FIT phase to ensure that role authorizations, restrictions, and business catalogs are functioning as expected.

Specifically, we would like to understand:

What is the recommended approach in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition for Test Users for testing purposes?
Are there SAP best practices for setting up test users during the Functional Integration Testing phase?

Our goal is to have one or more dedicated test users per custom role so that each role can be validated independently during manual testing.

We would appreciate any documentation, recommended procedures, or tools that SAP suggests for managing this scenario efficiently.

Thanks,

Ravi


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: CreateTest User ID for S/4HANA Public cloud
entry.content:
To effectively set up test users for the Functional Integration Testing phase in SAP S4HANA Cloud Public Edition consider the following best practices

  1. Create Test Users using the Manage Workforce
  2. Once a worker is created use the Maintain Business Users app to automatically create a business user and Assign Business Roles to User
  3. Test Each Role Individually

Utilize SAP Cloud Identity Services for managing authentication and user access, Refer Set Up Your SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition | SAP Help Portal

Refer documentation on Create a Business User in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition | SAP Help Portal  for detailed steps

entry.title: Accessibility - Dropdown lists
entry.content:
After an accessibility audit on SAP SuccessFactors, here are some non-compliances that were noted regarding dropdownlist, that we would appreciate to see evolve:

  • Up/Down arrow keys: navigate between list options

  • Space bar: select an option

  • The tabindex=1 attribute should not be used on interactive elements except in specific cases (which must be communicated to users for proper functionality).

entry.title: Re: Emarsys contact deduplication
entry.content:
Hello Joyca, 

thank you for your question and apologies for the delay. Hopefully this helps answer your question. 

Emarsys has a page to manage duplicate suspects: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_EMARSYS/f8e2fafeea804018a954a8857d9dfff3/fdf598a674c11014adc1d1efe5e1d939.html#managing-duplicate-suspects

However, we rely on customers to select the correct identifier upon import to avoid creating duplicate contacts.

The deduplication handling will leverage the Unique Identifier field selected as the key_id in API or the Unique Identifier field in the CSV import to ensure it does not create a duplicate contact with the same value for that Unique Identifier.

Additionally, here are some other resources:

  • a video explaining Data Requirements and Contact Identification.
  • This course could also be useful, specifically Unit 2, Lesson 2 

Please let us know if you have any further questions and thank you so much for attending our live sessions! 

Jessica Becker

entry.title: Understanding and choosing the appropriate Valuated SIT
entry.content:
Introduction

In today’s dynamic business environment, stock transfers between plants—whether within the same company code or across different codes—are a common practice. These movements are not just logistical necessities; they are strategic decisions aimed at optimizing operations, reducing excess inventory, and accelerating the alignment of costs with revenues.

Global B2B operations often involve affiliated or related companies, where the transfer of goods plays a crucial role in balancing supply and demand. Typically, high-value inventory is prioritized for such transfers, especially when the cost of shipping is significantly lower than the value of the goods themselves and when the shipping happens through multiple modes of transit across countries. Yet, one critical question always arises: who bears responsibility for the stock while it is in transit?

This concern lies at the heart of valuated stock in transit in SAP—a functionality designed to provide clarity, accountability, and financial accuracy during these crucial movements.

Solution options in SAP :

Valuated stock in transit is available in the following intra-company-code and cross-company-code processes.

1. Stock transfer with transfer of title at goods issue.

Stock transfer to valuated stock in transit of the receiving plant during goods issue by the supplying plant.

When to use -

When goods are shipped between different company codes, especially across countries, ownership often needs to transfer at the point of shipment.

This ensures that the selling company recognizes revenue, and the buying company recognizes the liability immediately.

Common in international trade under Incoterms like FOB (Free on Board) or EXW (Ex Works). Certain contracts stipulate that the buyer assumes responsibility once goods leave the seller’s premises.

Simplifies logistics when the receiving plant is far away or delivery times are long.

Avoids delays in financial postings since the transfer is recorded at goods issue.

STO Document Type: NBCR

Delivery type: NCCR

Item category: NCCR

Schedule line category: N1

Assigning schedule line category to item category: NCCR-N1

Defining Proof of delivery: NO

During VL02n – Material is transferred to Unrestricted stock of issuing plant to valuated stock in transit of receiving plant. – Transfer of title is completed.

During MIGO – Material is transferred from Valuated stock in transit of receiving plant to unrestricted stock of receiving plant using movement type 109.

Stock transfer is thereby completed.

2. Stock transfer with transfer of title at goods receipt.

Stock is transferred from valuated stock in transit of the supplying plant to directly to the unrestricted use stock of the receiving plant.

When to use-

Suitable when goods are shipped within the same region or country, where transit times are short and risks are minimal.

The issuing plant continues to bear responsibility until the receiving plant confirms receipt.

Certain agreements specify that ownership transfers only upon delivery.

Common under Incoterms like DAP (Delivered at Place) or DDP (Delivered Duty Paid), where the seller is responsible until goods reach the buyer’s location.

When the company wants the issuing plant to remain accountable for goods until they are physically received.

Helps avoid disputes if goods are damaged or lost in transit.

STO Document Type: NBC2

Delivery type: NCC2

Item category: NCC2

Schedule line category: N2

Assigning schedule line category to item category: NBC2-N2

Defining Proof of delivery: YES

During VL02n – Material is transferred to Unrestricted stock of issuing plant to valuated stock in transit of issuing plant.

During VLPOD – When the POD is confirmed, the stock is moved from the stock in transit of the issuing plant to the unrestricted use stock of the receiving plant. – Transfer of title is completed here, and the stock transfer ends thereby.

3.Stock transfer with transfer of title during transit.

This scenario in SAP is a middle ground between title transfer at goods issue and goods receipt. Here, ownership of the goods changes hands while the stock is physically in transit, at a defined point between shipment and delivery.

When to use –

This scenario in SAP is a middle ground between title transfer at goods issue and goods receipt. Here, ownership of the goods changes hands while the stock is physically in transit, at a defined point between shipment and delivery.

Common under Incoterms like CFR (Cost and Freight) or CIF (Cost, Insurance, Freight), where ownership transfers once goods cross a certain point (e.g., loaded on a vessel).

The buyer assumes ownership mid-transit, but the seller may still cover certain costs.

When goods are valuable and transit takes weeks (e.g., overseas shipping), companies may prefer to transfer title mid-transit to reflect the economic reality.

Allows both parties to recognize revenue and costs in the correct accounting period, especially when shipment and receipt fall in different months or quarters.

STO Document Type: NBC3

Delivery type: NCC3

Item category: NCC3

Schedule line category: N3

Assigning schedule line category to item category: NCC3-N3

Defining Proof of delivery: YES

During VL02n – Material is transferred to Unrestricted stock of issuing plant to valuated stock in transit of issuing plant.

During VLPOD – When the POD is confirmed, the stock is moved from the stock in transit of the issuing plant to the valuated stock in transit of the receiving plant. – Transfer of title happens here.

During MIGO – The movement type 109 is used, the stock is posted from valuated stock in transit of the receiving plant to the unrestricted use stock of the receiving plant. Thereby, completing the stock transfer.

Conclusion:

SAP provides multiple options for handling stock transfers—whether title passes at goods issue, goods receipt, or during transit. This flexibility is intentional, allowing businesses to align system behaviour with their accounting principles, contractual obligations, and the terms agreed between company codes.

However, the effectiveness of these options depends on clarity. A well-defined set of rules, agreed upon in advance, is essential before configuring the system. Without this, companies risk inconsistencies in ownership, valuation, and financial reporting.

By carefully analyzing all the impacting factors and setting clear rules upfront, organizations can ensure that SAP’s stock transfer functionality not only supports logistics but also strengthens financial accuracy and compliance.
